Important Notice: This Journal is only to be used by registered COSP™ Facilitators. Do not copy or distribute to others without permission.

Version 20.1

Getting Started

› Welcome to the COSP Registered Facilitator Fidelity Journal

Welcome to the COSP Registered Facilitator Fidelity Journal, a tool developed to support both the fidelity of implementation and the skill development of the facilitator. The reflective process required in the use of this Journal is central to the mission and methods of Circle of Security Parenting.

The Fidelity Journal has been developed based on years of learning from facilitators across the world. It is designed to deepen facilitator awareness and skill as you guide parents through the COSP curriculum. The Journal is based on the COSP Facilitator Core Competencies and is aligned with the Logic Model of the intervention.

This is a tool for your growth and development as a facilitator. As you engage in the reflective process outlined in this Journal, you will deepen your understanding of the material, recognize patterns in your facilitation, and develop greater skill and confidence in supporting parents to find their way to 'being with' their children in more secure ways.

› The Job of the COSP Facilitator

One helpful way to think about the job of the COSP Facilitator is to think of the Facilitator as the Hands for each caregiver with whom they work. Much like the parents in the Circle of Security, we as facilitators are called to "be bigger, stronger, wiser, and kind." Being the Hands means:

  • Bigger: Providing the necessary structure and holding the group in a way that supports safety and security for all caregivers.
  • Stronger: Having the strength and resolve to hold boundaries when needed and to stay true to the COSP curriculum and fidelity even when it feels uncomfortable.
  • Wiser: Using our understanding of attachment and reflective capacity to guide parents toward insight and understanding rather than giving advice or solving their problems.
  • Kind: Approaching parents with empathy, respect, and an underlying positive regard for their intentions and their capacity to change and grow.

Figure 1: The Circle of Security Diagram

As facilitators, we are also called to be aware of our own "Shark Music" — the feelings, thoughts, and reactions that get activated when we are with parents who are struggling or defensive. Our ability to be aware of our own experience and use it skillfully is central to our effectiveness as facilitators.

› COSP Facilitator Core Competencies

The COSP Facilitator Core Competencies provide a framework for understanding what skilled facilitation looks like. These competencies are organized into four areas:

1. State of Mind

The ability to maintain a reflective stance, to manage one's own "Shark Music," and to stay present and calm in the face of parents' struggles, defensiveness, or vulnerability.

2. Positive Intentionality

The capacity to find and hold the positive intentions of each caregiver, even when their behavior or words might suggest otherwise. This is foundational to creating the safety parents need to be vulnerable and open to learning.

3. Using the Circle of Security Roadmap

The ability to effectively use the framework and language of COS to help parents see their experience in new ways and to discover their own competence and the hidden capacities in their children's behaviors.

4. Reflective Capacity

The ability to engage in ongoing reflection about one's own facilitation, to be open to feedback, and to use reflection as a pathway to growth and development as a facilitator.

› COSP Logic Model

Every intervention is based on a Logic Model — a theory of how and why change happens. The COSP Logic Model describes how the program works to support parents in developing more secure relationships with their children.

The fundamental theory is that:

  • Parents bring their own experiences of being parented to their work with their children.
  • Many of the ways we parent are automatic — learned from our own childhood experiences.
  • Through a process of reflection, parents can become aware of these automatic patterns and begin to make different choices.
  • When parents develop the capacity to "be with" their children in ways that are reflective, attuned, and mentalizing, children develop secure internal working models of relationships.
  • Secure attachment relationships support children's development, learning, and wellbeing across all domains.
› How to Use This Journal

Use of the Fidelity Journal is twofold: (1) to reflect on your implementation of the COSP curriculum, including your fidelity to the program as it was designed, and (2) to reflect on your growth and development as a facilitator.

For each chapter, you will:

  • Part A — Reflection Page: Respond to a set of statements about your facilitation for that chapter. You will rate yourself on a 1-4 scale (1 = rarely, 4 = almost always). These statements address both your fidelity to the COSP curriculum and your skill development as a facilitator.
  • Part B — Reflective Diary: Write a reflective narrative about one key moment or learning from your facilitation of that chapter. You will be asked to select one of three topics to explore in your writing.
"I learned that I could really be quiet and that a lot of change could happen without me talking, that the power is not in how much information I give but in how I am with the parents."
— COSP Facilitator, USA, March 2017

As you use this Journal:

  • Approach your reflection with honesty and curiosity. There are no "right" answers.
  • Notice where you rate yourself lower — these are areas for growth and learning.
  • Use the reflective diary to explore moments that stood out to you, whether because they went well or because they were challenging.
  • If you are working with a COSP Fidelity Coach, bring your reflections to those sessions. Use the feedback you receive as a pathway to deeper understanding and skill development.
  • Remember: the goal of this work is not perfection, but growth. Each time you facilitate, you are developing greater skill and understanding.
"My experience in reflecting has been interesting. I have realized how much my own story affects my work, and how important it is to be aware of that. The reflective process has helped me become a more aware and intentional facilitator."
— COSP Facilitator, USA, April 2017
